text: Four trocars are placed in the left lateral quadrant: two for the robotic arms, a 12mm camera trocar, and one assistant port, establishing access points for the robotic hernia repair procedure.
Four trocars are placed in the left lateral quadrant: two for the robotic arms, a twelve millimeter camera trocar, and one assistant port.
The surgeon begins dissection using a monopolar hook to remove part of the hernia sac, leaving a small portion intact and transecting it at a high point.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. The monopolar hook is used in a bottom-up fashion for posterior dissection, simultaneously dissecting and retracting the tissue.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. For the posterior dissection, the monopolar hook is used in a bottom-up fashion, enabling simultaneous dissection and retraction.
Following the dissection of the hernia sac, a larger cavity is revealed, containing visible bowel loops.

The surgeon uses round tip eight millimeter scissors for precise dissection near the small bowel, minimizing the risk of energy spread to the surrounding serosa. The left hand gently opens the tissue plane, ensuring no injury to the small bowel.

The surgeon uses round tip eight millimeter scissors for careful dissection near the small bowel, minimizing the risk of energy spreading to the surrounding serosa. The left hand gently opens the tissue plane, ensuring no injury to the small bowel.

The surgeon encounters a complete fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, a common occurrence in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts. The dissection is performed cautiously due to the potential risks associated with separating these fused structures.

The surgeon encounters a complete fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, a condition often seen in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts, highlighting the potential dangers of dissection even with an open approach.
The surgeon utilizes a robotic platform to rotate a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees, enhancing the exposure of challenging planes during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic platform to rotate a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees, enhancing exposure to challenging planes within the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the defect. Small deserosalizations are repaired with interrupted stitches using PDS-IV-O suture, and a careful inspection reveals no deeper injuries.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the defect. Small deserosalizations are repaired using interrupted stitches of PDS-IV-O, with no deeper injuries observed upon careful inspection.
The surgeon approximates the two edges of the hernia defect using a short running suture with a size one V-lock barbed suture, effectively closing the defect.

The surgeon approximates the two edges of the hernia defect using a short running suture with a size one V-lock barbed suture, effectively closing the defect without the need for continuous traction from a fourth arm.
The surgeon carefully leaves an open passage for the small catheter to prevent obstruction, preparing to cover the area with mesh.
The surgeon ensures an open passage for the small catheter, avoiding obstruction by applying minimal force, and prepares to cover the area with mesh.

A 20x25 cm ventral light mesh is prepared with a longitudinal opening for the catheter. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement.

A 20x25 cm ventral light mesh is prepared with a longitudinal opening for the catheter. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement.

The surgeon creates a small opening around the catheter to prevent obstruction, then uses a Proline-II-O suture to close the mesh around the catheter, ensuring secure placement without compromising the catheter's function.
A small opening is created around the catheter to prevent obstruction, and a Proline-II-O suture is used to close the mesh around the catheter.
The surgeon employs a parachute technique to secure the mesh in one corner, compensating for the inability to place a stay suture, similar to techniques used for vascular structures.

The surgeon places short, 2-0 Prolene running sutures around the mesh to close the longitudinal opening, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned.
The surgeon places short, 2-0 Proline running sutures around the mesh to close the longitudinal opening, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned over the catheter.

The surgeon sutures the lateral quadrant of the mesh using a technique that involves a 180-degree rotation of the fingers on the needle control, achieving complete needle rotation for precise placement.
The surgeon sutures the lateral quadrant of the mesh using a technique that involves rotating the fingers 180 degrees on the control to achieve complete needle rotation.
